_owasp_mapping.A08:2025: A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures
_owasp_mapping.A09:2025: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Alerting Failures

_wp_path_patterns.description: WordPress path patterns that should be converted to FQD URLs when combined with a target domain
_wp_path_patterns.usage: Replace {DOMAIN} with target domain and append the path to create full URL
url_construction_rules: Remove trailing slashes from domain before appending path
Preserve query strings exactly as specified
For paths ending with /, ensure trailing slash is maintained

_rate_limiting_controls.description: Granular rate limiting controls for WordPress security testing and protection

global.description: Apply rate limit across all endpoints

per_endpoint.description: Apply rate limit per specific endpoint

per_ip.description: Apply rate limit per source IP address

per_user.description: Apply rate limit per authenticated user

baseline.expected_result: Some requests allowed, some blocked based on exact limit

overload.description: Test at 1.25x the configured rate limit

burst.expected_result: Burst should be detected and mitigated

distributed.expected_result: Each IP tracked independently if per_ip scope enabled

validation_rules: Requests exceeding limit must be blocked (HTTP 429) or throttled
Rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-*) should be present in responses
Retry-After header should be present when blocked

evidence_collection.description: Statistics and evidence collection for rate limiting

automatic.description: Automatic reset after time window expires

reset_confirmation.log_entry: Rate limits manually reset by {user} at {timestamp}

threshold_exceeded.description: Alert when rate limit threshold is exceeded

sustained_attack.description: Alert on sustained rate limit violations

_form_builder_support.description: Auto-detection and security controls for popular WordPress form builders

contact_form_7.name: Contact Form 7

special_handling.mail_headers: Prevent header injection by removing newlines

protection_steps: Install and configure 'Contact Form 7 Honeypot' or 'Really Simple CAPTCHA' add-on
Add reCAPTCHA v3 integration via 'Contact Form 7 reCAPTCHA' module
Enable 'Flamingo' plugin to store submissions in database instead of just email
Configure 'WPCF7_LOAD_JS' to false and load JS only on pages with forms
Use 'Contact Form 7 - Dynamic Text Extension' for server-side generated tokens
Implement custom validation hooks: add_filter('wpcf7_validate_*')
Add rate limiting via server config or security plugin (Wordfence/Sucuri)
Disable file uploads if not needed, or restrict to specific extensions
Use 'Contact Form 7 - Database' addon to log all submissions for audit
Configure SMTP properly to prevent email spoofing via 'WP Mail SMTP'

known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Update to latest version, restrict file uploads

testing_recommendations: Test form submission with empty required fields
Test with oversized files and disallowed extensions
Test email header injection in name/email fields
Verify CAPTCHA cannot be bypassed
Test rate limiting by submitting multiple forms rapidly

elementor_forms.name: Elementor Forms
protection_steps: Enable Elementor built-in 'Honeypot Field' in form settings
Add reCAPTCHA v3 or hCaptcha to all forms via Elementor integrations
Configure 'Actions After Submit' to validate data before email/webhook
Use 'Webhook' action with signature verification for external APIs
Enable 'Collect Submissions' to store in database for audit trail
Restrict file upload fields to specific MIME types and size limits
Use 'Conditional Logic' to show/hide sensitive fields based on validation
Implement custom validation via 'elementor_pro/forms/validation' hook
Add rate limiting at server level for /wp-json/elementor-pro/v1/forms/*
Disable 'PDF Submission' feature if not needed (security risk)
Regularly update Elementor Pro to patch vulnerabilities
Use OOPSpam or similar plugin for advanced rate limiting and spam protection [^8^]

known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Update Elementor Pro, restrict PDF access

testing_recommendations: Test form submission bypass without JavaScript
Test webhook payload manipulation
Verify redirect URLs cannot be manipulated
Test file upload with PHP double extensions
Verify PDF access controls if feature enabled

gravity_forms.name: Gravity Forms

special_handling.file_uploads: Use gform_upload_path filter to change upload directory
special_handling.post_creation: Validate post_content for XSS before wp_insert_post
special_handling.user_registration: Enforce strong password policy via gform_user_registration_validation

protection_steps: Enable built-in 'Anti-Spam Honeypot' in Form Settings > General
Configure Cloudflare Turnstile or reCAPTCHA v3 (recommended: Turnstile) [^1^]
Enable Akismet integration for server-side spam detection
Restrict file upload extensions strictly - never leave blank [^1^]
Set maximum file size limits appropriate to use case
Require authentication for file uploads if possible
Verify .htaccess exists in wp-content/uploads/gravity_forms/ with 'php_flag engine off'
Use gform_upload_root_htaccess_rules filter to customize upload protection
Enable 'Data Retention' policies to auto-delete old entries (GDPR compliance)
Disable IP collection for forms where not needed (Form Settings > Personal Data)
Use 'Advanced Permissions' plugin for role-based form access control [^11^]
Implement gform_validation hook for custom server-side validation
Enable 'State Validation' for pricing/payment fields to prevent tampering
Regularly update Gravity Forms - critical patches for file upload vulnerabilities [^1^]
Monitor for supply chain attacks by verifying checksums after updates [^7^]
Use Gravity Forms 'Webhooks' with signature verification only

known_vulnerabilities.description: Arbitrary file upload via .phar files in chunked upload
known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Update to 2.9.22+, disable .phar execution at server level
Update immediately, restrict post creation forms

testing_recommendations: Test file upload with .phar, .php5, .phtml extensions
Test merge tag injection: {post_meta:user_email} in form fields
Verify chunked upload endpoint security
Test post creation form for unauthorized post status changes
Check webhook payload validation and signature verification
Verify entry export permissions (should require 'gravityforms_export_entries')

ninja_forms.name: Ninja Forms

special_handling.merge_tags: CRITICAL: Sanitize repeater field merge tags to prevent info disclosure (CVE-2026-2268) [^10^]
special_handling.ajax_submissions: Validate nonce on all nf_ajax_submit requests

protection_steps: Update to version 3.14.1+ immediately to patch CVE-2026-2268 [^10^]
Enable 'Ninja Forms Anti-Spam' setting with honeypot field
Add reCAPTCHA v3 field to all public forms
Disable repeater fields if not absolutely needed (high risk)
Implement WAF rule to block {post_meta: patterns in submissions [^10^]
Add robots.txt: 'Disallow: /wp-content/uploads/ninja-forms/' [^9^]
Create .htaccess in uploads folder with 'X-Robots-Tag noindex, nofollow' [^9^]
Use 'Ninja Forms - File Uploads' with strict extension whitelist
Enable 'Save Submissions' to database for audit trail
Configure SMTP properly - use 'WP Mail SMTP' plugin
Implement custom validation via 'ninja_forms_submit_data' hook
Add rate limiting at server level for admin-ajax.php?action=nf_ajax_submit
Regular security audits - check for unauthorized merge tag usage
Use 'Ninja Forms - User Management' for role-based access control

known_vulnerabilities.description: Sensitive Information Exposure via repeater field merge tags
known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Update to 3.14.1+, disable repeater fields, implement WAF rules

testing_recommendations: Test repeater field with {post_meta:user_email} merge tag injection
Verify file uploads are not accessible directly via URL
Test AJAX submission without valid nonce
Check for information disclosure in form preview mode
Verify submission data is properly sanitized in database

wordpress_core_forms.name: WordPress Core Native Forms
wordpress_core_forms.description: WordPress native authentication and API endpoints - always present, high criticality

login_form.name: WordPress Login Form
protection_steps: Implement strong rate limiting (5 attempts per 5 minutes)
Use uniform error messages: 'Invalid credentials' (prevents enumeration)
Enable 2FA/MFA via plugin (Wordfence, Two Factor)
Add CAPTCHA after 2 failed attempts
Consider renaming wp-login.php or IP-restricting access
Implement login attempt logging and alerting
Use HTTPS only for credential submission
Enable 'Secure cookie' flag for authentication cookies
Implement session timeout and concurrent session control
Block known malicious IP ranges at server level

known_vulnerabilities.description: Username enumeration via different error messages
known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Use uniform error messages, rename login URL

testing_recommendations: Test with valid vs invalid usernames - verify same error message
Test brute force rate limiting triggers
Test session fixation after login
Verify password is not logged anywhere
Test for SQL injection in username field
Test redirect_to parameter for open redirect
Verify no autocomplete on password in shared environments

password_reset.name: WordPress Password Reset Form
protection_steps: Strict rate limiting (2 per minute) to prevent email flooding
Add CAPTCHA to password reset form
Use uniform response regardless of user existence
Implement token expiration (24 hours max)
Send reset email asynchronously to prevent timing attacks
Log all reset requests with IP and timestamp
Alert admin on unusual reset volume
Invalidate old tokens when new one requested
Verify Host header to prevent reset poisoning
Use cryptographically secure token generation

known_vulnerabilities.description: Email flooding via rapid reset requests

testing_recommendations: Test email flooding with automated requests
Verify same response for valid/invalid usernames
Test token expiration and reuse
Check for token in URL vs email only
Test for Host header injection (password reset poisoning)
Verify reset token entropy (minimum 128 bits)
Test for user enumeration via timing differences

registration.name: WordPress User Registration Form
protection_steps: Require email verification before account activation
Implement CAPTCHA on registration form
Enforce strong password policy on registration
Prevent registration with privileged usernames (admin, etc.)
Rate limit by IP and email domain
Enable admin approval for new registrations if appropriate
Validate email domain against disposable email blacklist
Check for email reputation before sending verification
Implement honeypot fields for bot detection
Log all registrations with IP for audit trail
Auto-delete unverified accounts after 7 days

known_vulnerabilities.description: Privilege escalation via role parameter injection
known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Keep WordPress updated, validate role server-side

testing_recommendations: Test for role parameter injection in POST data
Test for username enumeration during registration
Verify email verification flow
Test CAPTCHA bypass
Check for disposable email acceptance
Test rate limiting on registration
Verify strong password enforcement

rest_api.name: WordPress REST API Endpoints
protection_steps: Require authentication for user enumeration endpoints
Disable REST API completely if not needed (plugins available)
Implement granular capability checks on all endpoints
Add custom authentication for sensitive operations
Log all REST API requests with IP and user agent
Rate limit unauthenticated requests aggressively
Block known scanning tools and user agents
Implement CORS policy strictly
Validate and sanitize all query parameters
Use application passwords instead of basic auth
Enable nonce verification for all write operations

known_vulnerabilities.mitigation: Require auth for users endpoint, or disable

testing_recommendations: Test all endpoints without authentication
Test for IDOR vulnerabilities in resource access
Verify proper capability checks on admin endpoints
Test for parameter pollution attacks
Check for information disclosure in error messages
Test rate limiting on API endpoints
Verify CORS configuration
Test for mass assignment vulnerabilities

xml_rpc.name: WordPress XML-RPC Interface
protection_steps: RECOMMENDED: Disable XML-RPC completely via .htaccess
If needed: Block specific dangerous methods (pingback.ping, system.multicall)
Implement strict rate limiting if enabled
Log all XML-RPC access attempts
Block requests to xmlrpc.php at server/WAF level
Use application passwords if XML-RPC auth required
Monitor for brute force amplification attacks
Disable pingback functionality in WordPress settings
testing_recommendations: Test if xmlrpc.php is accessible
Test pingback.ping method for amplification
Test system.multicall for brute force amplification
Verify system.listMethods returns methods
Test for XXE injection in XML parsing
Check for information disclosure in error messages
Verify authentication bypass vulnerabilities

methods.description: Check if plugin is in active_plugins option
Scan post_content for form builder shortcodes
Scan page source for form-specific CSS classes
Check if REST API endpoints return valid responses
Verify plugin files exist in wp-content/plugins/

unified_protection_layer.description: Protection measures that apply to all form builders

global_filters: Strip all HTML tags except allowed list
Remove JavaScript event handlers (onerror, onload, etc.)
Normalize unicode to prevent homograph attacks
Block known spam patterns and keywords

integration_with_rate_limiting.description: How form builder protection integrates with global rate limiting

core_forms_detection.description: Detection methods for WordPress core forms (always present, no detection needed)

features.name: wp-cron.php Enabled Leads to DoS Attack
features.description: WordPress cron system enabled via wp-cron.php allows resource exhaustion through repeated cron spam requests, leading to server overload.
verification_steps: 2. Monitor server load during repeated requests
3. Check cron event logs for spam triggers
features.remediation: Disable wp-cron.php via wp-config.php (define('DISABLE_WP_CRON', true)); Use server cron jobs instead.

features.name: Xmlrpc enabled Leads to Ping back attack
features.description: XML-RPC enabled allows distributed pingback amplification attacks, enabling DDoS via pingback.ping method.
verification_steps: 4. Check server logs for XML-RPC access
features.remediation: Disable XML-RPC via .htaccess or plugins like Disable XML-RPC. Block /xmlrpc.php entirely.

features.name: Username Enumeration via WordPress REST API
features.description: WP REST API /wp/v2/users endpoint exposes usernames without authentication.
features.remediation: Add .htaccess rule: RewriteRule ^wp-json/wp/v2/users - [F]. Use authentication plugins.

features.name: Email Flooding via Password Reset
features.description: wp-login.php?checkemail=1 endpoint abuse allows unlimited password reset emails.
features.remediation: Implement rate limiting via plugins (Limit Login Attempts). Add CAPTCHA to reset form.

features.name: Exposed WordPress Admin Username via Author Query
features.description: /?author=1 reveals admin usernames through IDOR in author archives.
features.remediation: Add .htaccess: RewriteCond %{QUERY_STRING} ^author=.* [NC] RewriteRule .* - [R=403]. Rename admin user.

features.name: Endpoint Disclosure (auto-generated WP REST routes)
features.description: WordPress auto-generates REST API routes exposing internal endpoints without permission checks.
features.remediation: Disable public REST endpoints or use authentication middleware.

features.name: Lack of Rate Limiting on WordPress Login
features.description: wp-login.php lacks brute-force protection, enabling unlimited login attempts.
features.remediation: Install Limit Login Attempts Reloaded. Add fail2ban rules for wp-login.php.

features.name: Username Enumeration via wp-login.php
features.description: Different error messages on wp-login.php reveal valid vs invalid usernames.
verification_steps: 2. POST known username with bad pass
features.remediation: Use uniform error: 'Invalid credentials'. Plugins: Rename wp-login.php.

features.name: Lack of Rate Limiting on Contact and Registration Forms
features.description: Contact/registration forms allow spam/DoS without submission limits.
features.remediation: Add reCAPTCHA v3. Rate limit via server config or plugins like WPForms Smart Tags.

features.name: Server Banner Grabbing
features.description: Server headers expose Apache/Nginx versions for targeted exploits.
features.remediation: Apache: ServerTokens Prod. Nginx: server_tokens off;.htaccess: Header unset Server.

features.name: Information Disclosure via readme.html
features.description: Root readme.html exposes exact WordPress version to attackers.
features.remediation: .htaccess: <Files readme.html> Order allow,deny Deny from all </Files>. Delete file.

features.name: HSTS Not Implemented
features.description: Missing HSTS header enables HTTPS downgrade and MITM attacks.
features.remediation: Add to .htaccess: Header always set Strict-Transport-Security 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains'. Use security plugins.

features.name: WordPress Version Disclosure
features.description: Checks if the WordPress version is publicly visible via generator tags, readme files, or error messages, which can help attackers target known vulnerabilities.
verification_steps: 1. View page source and search for 'generator' meta tag
2. Access /readme.html and check for version number
3. Trigger error by accessing non-existent page, check if version appears
4. Use wpscan or similar tool: `wpscan --url http://example.com --enumerate vp`
5. Check REST API endpoints: `/wp-json/wp/v2` may return version
features.remediation: Add to wp-config.php: `remove_action('wp_head', 'wp_generator');`. Delete readme.html. Configure server to not reveal version in errors.

features.name: Weak Password Policy Enforcement
features.description: Tests if the application enforces strong password policies including minimum length, complexity requirements, and prevents common passwords.
verification_steps: 1. Attempt to register or change password with weak passwords (123456, password, qwerty)
2. Test minimum length requirement by trying passwords under 8 characters
3. Test complexity: try passwords without numbers, special characters, or mixed case
4. Check if password strength meter enforces requirements
5. Attempt to reuse old passwords during password change
6. Use Burp Suite Intruder with common passwords list
features.remediation: Implement strong password policy (min 12 chars, complexity). Use password strength meter. Consider password blacklist. Implement password history (prevent reuse).

features.name: SQL Injection Protection
features.description: Verifies that all user inputs are properly sanitized and parameterized queries or prepared statements are used to prevent SQL injection attacks.
verification_steps: 1. Test all input fields with SQLi payloads: `' OR '1'='1`, `" OR "1"="1`
4. Test UNION-based injections: `' UNION SELECT null,username,password FROM users--`
5. Test error-based: `' AND 1=CAST((SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables) AS INT)--`
7. Check for database errors in response
features.remediation: Use prepared statements with parameterized queries. Sanitize all inputs with `$wpdb->prepare()`. Implement input validation. Use WordPress security plugins with SQLi protection.

features.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Protection
features.description: Tests for proper input sanitization and output escaping to prevent reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS attacks.
verification_steps: 1. Test all input fields with basic XSS payload: `<script>alert('XSS')</script>`
2. Test for stored XSS in comments, user profiles, posts
3. Test reflected XSS in search, contact forms, URL parameters
5. Test SVG file upload with XSS payload
6. Test event handlers: `onload`, `onerror`, `onclick` in HTML attributes
7. Use OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite active scanner
8. Check if output is properly escaped with `esc_html()`, `esc_attr()`
features.remediation: Use `esc_html()`, `esc_attr()`, `esc_url()` for output. Validate and sanitize inputs with `sanitize_text_field()`. Implement Content Security Policy (CSP).

features.name: XML External Entity (XXE) Protection
features.description: Checks if XML processors are configured to prevent XXE attacks by disabling external entity processing.
verification_steps: 1. Identify XML input points (file uploads, API endpoints)
2. Test with malicious XML: `<?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE root [<!ENTITY test SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root>&test;</root>`
3. Test for out-of-band XXE using external domains
6. Use XXEinjector tool for comprehensive testing
features.remediation: Disable XML external entity processing. Use JSON instead of XML where possible. Use PHP's `libxml_disable_entity_loader(true)`. Keep XML processors updated.

features.name: Broken Access Control
features.description: Tests for insecure direct object references, missing function-level access controls, and privilege escalation vulnerabilities.
features.test_method: Manual testing with privilege escalation attempts
verification_steps: 1. Test IDOR by changing numeric IDs in URLs: `/user/123` â†’ `/user/124`
2. Test parameter tampering in POST/PUT requests
3. Test admin functionality without admin role: `/wp-admin/` access
4. Test API endpoints without proper authentication
5. Check if users can edit others' content
6. Test direct access to uploaded files without permission
7. Test CSRF on state-changing actions (see separate CSRF test)
features.remediation: Implement proper role-based access control (RBAC). Validate permissions on every request. Use WordPress capabilities system properly. Implement CSRF tokens.

features.name: Security Misconfiguration
features.description: Checks for default configurations, verbose error messages, unnecessary services, and insecure HTTP headers.
verification_steps: 2. Verify debug mode is disabled: `wp config get WP_DEBUG`
4. Check HTTP headers with securityheaders.com or `curl -I`
7. Test for default database table prefix 'wp_'
features.remediation: Harden WordPress configuration. Remove unused plugins/themes. Secure file permissions. Implement security headers. Regular security audits.

features.name: Insecure Deserialization
features.description: Tests for insecure deserialization of user-supplied data that could lead to remote code execution or privilege escalation.
features.owasp: A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures
verification_steps: 1. Identify serialized data in cookies, parameters, or files
3. Test with ysoserial or similar exploit generation tools
4. Look for `unserialize()` function usage in code
5. Test file upload with serialized payloads
6. Check session handling for serialized data
features.remediation: Avoid unserializing user input. Use JSON instead of serialization. Implement digital signatures on serialized data. Use safe deserialization libraries.

features.name: Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities
features.description: Identifies outdated WordPress core, themes, and plugins with known CVEs and security vulnerabilities.
verification_steps: 1. Run `wp core version` to check WordPress version
5. Check for known vulnerabilities with CVE databases
6. Use retire.js for JavaScript library vulnerabilities
7. Test with nikto or similar vulnerability scanners
features.remediation: Regular updates of core, plugins, themes. Remove unused components. Subscribe to security bulletins. Use vulnerability scanners regularly.

features.name: Insufficient Logging & Monitoring
features.description: Verifies that security events are properly logged, monitored, and alerts are configured for suspicious activities.
features.owasp: A09:2025 - Security Logging and Alerting Failures
verification_steps: 1. Check if failed login attempts are logged
2. Test if admin actions are recorded
4. Check for SQL query logging in debug mode
5. Test if brute force attempts trigger alerts
6. Check log rotation and retention policies
7. Verify logs are stored securely (not in web root)
features.remediation: Implement comprehensive logging with plugins like WP Security Audit Log. Set up log monitoring. Configure alerts for suspicious activities. Regular log review.

features.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Protection
features.description: Tests for presence of anti-CSRF tokens in state-changing requests and proper validation mechanisms.
verification_steps: 1. Identify state-changing actions (post creation, user update, plugin activation)
2. Check for nonce tokens in forms: look for `_wpnonce` or `nonce` parameters
3. Test without token: remove nonce and submit form
4. Test with invalid token: change nonce value
5. Check if token is tied to user session and action
6. Test token reuse: use same token twice
7. Use Burp Suite to test for CSRF vulnerabilities
features.remediation: Use WordPress nonces (`wp_nonce_field()`, `wp_nonce_url()`). Implement SameSite cookies. Add CSRF protection to custom forms. Use framework CSRF protection.

features.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
features.description: Checks for vulnerabilities that allow attackers to make requests from the server to internal resources or external domains.
verification_steps: 1. Test URL parameters that fetch external resources
6. Test for blind SSRF using Burp Collaborator
7. Check XML import functionality for XXE-based SSRF
features.remediation: Validate and sanitize URL inputs. Use allowlists for domains/IPs. Implement network segmentation. Disable unused URL schemes. Use cloud metadata protection.

features.name: File Upload Security
features.description: Tests file upload functionality for proper file type validation, content checking, and secure storage.
verification_steps: 3. Test content-type bypass: change Content-Type header
4. Upload malicious files with valid image headers (Polyglot files)
6. Check if files are stored outside web root
features.remediation: Validate file type by content, not extension. Use allowlists for file types. Store files outside web root. Use secure file permissions. Scan uploaded files.

features.name: Directory Traversal
features.description: Tests for vulnerabilities that allow reading or writing files outside the web root directory.
verification_steps: 2. Test path traversal with encoded characters: `..%2f..%2f`
6. Test in log file viewing features
7. Use dot-dot-slash payloads with various encodings
features.remediation: Validate file paths. Use basename() to extract filename. Implement path canonicalization. Use allowlists for accessible files. Web server hardening.

features.name: Admin Interface Protection
features.description: Verifies that WordPress admin interface is properly protected with strong authentication, IP restrictions, and rate limiting.
verification_steps: 3. Test brute force on login page
4. Check if admin area uses HTTPS
7. Test session timeout in admin area
8. Verify admin email is not publicly accessible
features.remediation: Implement strong passwords + 2FA. Rename admin path or IP restrict. Use HTTPS. Implement login attempt limiting. Regular admin session timeout.

features.name: XML-RPC API Security
features.description: Tests the XML-RPC interface for vulnerabilities including pingback DDoS amplification, brute force attacks, and remote code execution.
verification_steps: 1. Check if XML-RPC is enabled: `curl -X POST -d '<methodCall><methodName>system.listMethods</methodName></methodCall>' http://site.com/xmlrpc.php`
5. Test for XXE in XML-RPC requests
features.remediation: Disable XML-RPC if not needed: add to .htaccess `RewriteRule ^xmlrpc\.php$ - [F,L]`. Use security plugins to block abuse. Implement rate limiting.

features.name: REST API Endpoint Security
features.description: Tests WordPress REST API endpoints for proper authentication, authorization, and input validation.
verification_steps: 2. Test unauthenticated access to protected endpoints
6. Test injection vulnerabilities in API parameters
7. Test rate limiting on API endpoints
features.remediation: Disable REST API if not used. Require authentication for sensitive endpoints. Implement proper permissions. Add rate limiting. Use API security plugins.

features.name: User Enumeration
features.description: Tests for user enumeration vulnerabilities via author archives, login error messages, or REST API endpoints.
verification_steps: 1. Check author archives: `/author/1/`, try incrementing IDs
2. Test login page error messages with valid/invalid usernames
4. Test registration page for existing username check
6. Test comment feeds for author information
features.remediation: Disable author archives or use numeric IDs. Use generic login error messages. Restrict REST API user endpoint. Use security plugins to prevent enumeration.

features.name: Brute Force Protection
features.description: Verifies implementation of account lockout, CAPTCHA, and rate limiting mechanisms for login attempts.
verification_steps: 1. Test multiple failed login attempts on same account
2. Test distributed attacks from different IPs
3. Check if lockout duration is reasonable
8. Use Hydra or similar tools (with permission)
features.remediation: Implement account lockout after X attempts. Use CAPTCHA. Add rate limiting per IP. Use 2FA. Monitor failed login attempts. Use WordPress security plugins.

features.name: Session Management Security
features.description: Tests for secure session handling including proper timeout, regeneration, and secure cookie attributes.
verification_steps: 1. Check cookie attributes: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite
2. Test session fixation: login with known session ID
4. Check if session regenerates on login
8. Use Burp Suite to analyze session cookies
features.remediation: Set Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies. Implement session timeout. Regenerate session IDs on privilege change. Store sessions securely. Limit concurrent sessions.

features.name: HTTP Security Headers
features.description: Checks for presence of security headers like Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, etc.
verification_steps: 8. Test caching headers for sensitive content
features.remediation: Add security headers via .htaccess or plugin. Use CSP with proper directives. Implement HSTS. Set secure Referrer-Policy. Regular header testing.

features.name: SSL/TLS Configuration
features.description: Tests for proper SSL/TLS configuration including protocol versions, cipher suites, and certificate validity.
verification_steps: 3. Test for SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1 support
4. Check cipher strength and weak ciphers
features.remediation: Disable old protocols (SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0). Use strong cipher suites. Implement HSTS. Renew certificates before expiration. Use Qualys SSL Test regularly.

features.name: Database Security Configuration
features.description: Verifies secure database configuration including proper permissions, encryption, and connection security.
verification_steps: 1. Check database user permissions (SELECT only needed)
2. Verify database connection uses SSL if remote
3. Check if database credentials are in wp-config.php
6. Verify table prefix is not default 'wp_'
8. Check database version for known vulnerabilities
features.remediation: Use least privilege database users. Encrypt database connections. Secure wp-config.php. Change default table prefix. Regular database updates and patches.

features.name: File Permissions
features.description: Checks for proper file and directory permissions on WordPress files, preventing unauthorized read/write access.
verification_steps: 4. Test for writable PHP files in web root
features.remediation: Set proper permissions: directories 755, files 644, wp-config.php 640. Use correct ownership. Regular permission audits. Use security plugins to check.

features.name: wp-config.php Protection
features.description: Verifies that wp-config.php file is properly protected and contains secure configuration settings.
verification_steps: 5. Verify WP_DEBUG is false in production
6. Check for database table prefix customization
7. Test for backup files: wp-config.php.bak, wp-config.php.save
features.remediation: Move wp-config.php one level above web root if possible. Set proper file permissions. Use unique security keys. Disable file editor. Remove backup files.

features.name: .htaccess Security Rules
features.description: Tests for presence of security hardening rules in .htaccess or web server configuration.
verification_steps: 1. Check if .htaccess exists in WordPress root
4. Verify XML-RPC blocking if not used
6. Test for PHP execution in uploads directory
8. Check for user agent blocking if applicable
features.remediation: Implement security hardening in .htaccess. Block directory listing. Restrict file access. Disable XML-RPC if unused. Add security headers. Regular .htaccess review.

features.name: Debug Mode Exposure
features.description: Checks if WordPress debug mode is enabled in production, potentially exposing sensitive information.
verification_steps: 2. Trigger PHP errors and check for detailed messages
4. Look for `define('WP_DEBUG_LOG', true);` and log location
5. Test with invalid query parameter to trigger errors
6. Check if debug information appears in page source
7. Use WordPress CLI: `wp config get WP_DEBUG`
features.remediation: Set `WP_DEBUG` to false in production. Use `WP_DEBUG_LOG` for debugging but not `WP_DEBUG_DISPLAY`. Remove debug information from production. Regular config review.

features.name: Database Error Disclosure
features.description: Tests for database error messages exposed to users, which could reveal sensitive information.
verification_steps: 1. Trigger SQL errors with invalid queries
3. Check for detailed error messages in response
5. Check if errors are logged instead of displayed
7. Check AJAX endpoints for error disclosure
8. Monitor network traffic for error details
features.remediation: Set `display_errors` to Off in php.ini. Configure WordPress to log errors instead of displaying. Use custom error pages. Implement proper error handling.

features.name: PHP Error Reporting
features.description: Verifies that PHP error reporting is disabled in production environment.
verification_steps: 1. Create a PHP file with `<?php echo ini_get('display_errors'); ?>`
3. Test with syntax error in PHP files
6. Test with division by zero or undefined variables
features.remediation: Set `display_errors = Off` in php.ini. Set `error_reporting` to appropriate level. Use `ini_set()` in code if needed. Regular PHP configuration review.

features.name: Directory Listing
features.description: Tests for enabled directory listing which could expose sensitive files and directory structure.
verification_steps: 5. Verify server responds with directory listing
7. Use DirBuster or similar directory brute-forcing tools
features.remediation: Add `Options -Indexes` to .htaccess. Ensure each directory has index.php or index.html. Configure web server to disable directory listing. Regular directory checks.

features.name: Backup File Exposure
features.description: Scans for exposed backup files (.bak, .backup, .sql, etc.) containing sensitive data.
verification_steps: 5. Use tools like DirBuster with backup file wordlists
features.remediation: Remove backup files from web root. Store backups in secure locations. Use .htaccess to block backup file access. Implement proper backup procedures outside web root.

features.name: Configuration File Exposure
features.description: Tests for exposed configuration files (config.php, configuration.php, etc.) with database credentials.
verification_steps: 6. Look for `robots.txt` that may reveal sensitive paths
7. Use automated scanners for configuration files
features.remediation: Move sensitive config files outside web root. Set proper file permissions (600). Use environment variables for credentials. Regular security scans for exposed files.

features.name: Log File Exposure
features.description: Checks for exposed log files containing sensitive information about users and system activities.
verification_steps: 1. Check for common log file locations
7. Use directory traversal to access log files
features.remediation: Disable public access to log files via .htaccess. Store logs outside web root. Regularly rotate and delete old logs. Use secure logging plugins.

features.name: Source Code Disclosure
features.description: Tests for source code disclosure vulnerabilities through misconfigured servers or file handling.
verification_steps: 1. Test for PHP source disclosure via path manipulation
3. Test file inclusion with PHP filters
5. Look for `.svn` or other version control files
6. Test for LFI vulnerabilities that reveal source code
7. Check for misconfigured servers exposing source
features.remediation: Configure web server to parse PHP files properly. Remove version control directories. Secure file inclusion. Regular source code disclosure checks.

features.name: Clickjacking Protection
features.description: Verifies implementation of X-Frame-Options or Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directives.
verification_steps: 2. Verify X-Frame-Options is set to DENY or SAMEORIGIN
5. Verify admin pages cannot be framed
6. Use browser developer tools to test framing
features.remediation: Implement X-Frame-Options: DENY. Use CSP frame-ancestors directive. Add frame-busting JavaScript for older browsers. Test clickjacking protection regularly.

features.name: MIME Type Sniffing Protection
features.description: Tests for X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header to prevent MIME type sniffing attacks.
verification_steps: 3. Test with different file types and Content-Type headers
4. Check if browsers respect the header
6. Test upload functionality with mismatched MIME types
7. Verify all resources serve with correct Content-Type
features.remediation: Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header. Ensure correct Content-Type for all resources. Test MIME type handling. Regular header verification.

features.name: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
features.description: Tests for misconfigured CORS policies that could expose sensitive data to unauthorized domains.
verification_steps: 2. Test if wildcard (*) is used improperly
6. Test different HTTP methods with CORS
7. Use browser developer tools for CORS testing
features.remediation: Implement strict CORS policies. Avoid wildcard origins. Validate origin headers. Handle preflight requests properly. Test CORS configuration.

features.name: Subresource Integrity (SRI)
features.description: Checks for Subresource Integrity implementation on externally loaded scripts and stylesheets.
verification_steps: 1. Check script and link tags for integrity attribute
2. Verify external resources have SRI hashes
3. Test if SRI prevents tampered resource loading
4. Check for crossorigin attribute with SRI
5. Use browser developer tools to verify SRI
7. Check CDN resources for SRI support
features.remediation: Implement SRI for all external scripts/styles. Generate integrity hashes. Use crossorigin attribute. Regularly update SRI hashes when resources change.

features.name: Content Security Policy (CSP)
features.description: Tests for proper Content Security Policy implementation to mitigate XSS and data injection attacks.
verification_steps: 2. Verify CSP directives are properly configured
3. Test for overly permissive policies (unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval)
4. Check CSP reporting with report-uri or report-to
features.remediation: Implement strict CSP policy. Avoid unsafe directives. Use nonces or hashes for inline scripts. Enable CSP reporting. Test CSP regularly.

features.name: Referrer Policy
features.description: Verifies proper Referrer-Policy header implementation to control referrer information leakage.
verification_steps: 3. Test referrer leakage with external links
4. Check meta tag implementation if header not used
5. Use browser developer tools to monitor referrers
6. Test sensitive pages for referrer information
7. Verify policy applies to all resources
features.remediation: Implement Referrer-Policy header. Use strict policy (no-referrer or same-origin). Test referrer leakage. Regular policy review.

features.name: Feature Policy / Permissions Policy
features.description: Tests for Feature-Policy header to restrict browser features and APIs.
verification_steps: 1. Check for Feature-Policy or Permissions-Policy header
2. Verify restrictive policies for sensitive features
4. Check for fullscreen, payment, notifications policies
5. Use browser developer tools to test policies
7. Test with feature policy testing tools
features.remediation: Implement restrictive Feature-Policy. Disable unnecessary browser features. Use Permissions-Policy for modern browsers. Test policy effectiveness.

features.name: Insecure HTTP Methods
features.description: Tests for enabled dangerous HTTP methods like PUT, DELETE, TRACE, which could be abused.
verification_steps: 1. Use OPTIONS method to discover allowed methods
2. Test PUT method for file upload vulnerabilities
3. Test DELETE method for file removal
4. Test TRACE method for XST attacks
5. Check for WebDAV methods if enabled
7. Test each method with authentication bypass attempts
features.remediation: Disable unnecessary HTTP methods. Allow only GET, POST, HEAD. Use .htaccess to restrict methods. Implement proper authentication for all methods.

features.name: HTTP Verb Tampering
features.description: Tests for authentication/authorization bypass using different HTTP methods.
verification_steps: 1. Test protected endpoints with different HTTP methods
2. Try GET instead of POST on authenticated actions
3. Test HEAD method for information disclosure
4. Check if authentication checks are method-dependent
6. Use Burp Suite to tamper with request methods
7. Test API endpoints with various methods
features.remediation: Implement consistent authentication across all methods. Validate methods properly. Use framework routing correctly. Test all endpoints with different methods.

features.name: Cache Poisoning
features.description: Tests for web cache poisoning vulnerabilities through improper cache configuration.
verification_steps: 1. Test for unkeyed input in requests
7. Use tools like param miner to find unkeyed inputs
features.remediation: Configure cache keys properly. Validate and sanitize headers. Implement proper cache-control. Use CDN security features. Regular cache configuration review.

features.name: Host Header Injection
features.description: Tests for host header injection vulnerabilities leading to password reset poisoning or cache poisoning.
verification_steps: 4. Check for host override in URLs
6. Verify email links use correct host
7. Test cache poisoning via host headers
features.remediation: Validate Host header against allowlist. Use SERVER_NAME instead of Host where possible. Implement proper canonical URLs. Secure password reset functionality.

features.name: Open Redirect
features.description: Tests for open redirect vulnerabilities that could be used in phishing attacks.
verification_steps: 1. Test URL parameters for redirects (redirect, return, url)
2. Test with external domains in redirect parameters
7. Use automated scanners for open redirects
features.remediation: Validate redirect URLs. Use allowlist for redirect domains. Implement redirect confirmation pages. Use relative URLs where possible.

features.name: Local File Inclusion (LFI)
features.description: Tests for local file inclusion vulnerabilities through improper file path handling.
verification_steps: 1. Test file parameters with directory traversal
features.remediation: Validate file paths strictly. Use allowlists for includable files. Disable dangerous PHP wrappers. Implement proper file permission controls.

features.name: Remote File Inclusion (RFI)
features.description: Tests for remote file inclusion vulnerabilities allowing inclusion of external malicious files.
verification_steps: 1. Test file parameters with external URLs
2. Try including files from attacker-controlled servers
3. Test with different protocols (http, ftp, php://)
5. Test for RFI to RCE conversion
features.remediation: Disable allow_url_fopen and allow_url_include. Validate file paths. Use allowlists. Implement WAF rules for RFI. Regular code review for inclusion functions.

features.name: Command Injection
features.description: Tests for command injection vulnerabilities through system command execution with user input.
verification_steps: 1. Test input fields with command separators: `;`, `|`, `&`
7. Test with blind command injection payloads
features.remediation: Avoid shell command execution with user input. Use built-in PHP functions instead of shell commands. If needed, use `escapeshellarg()`. Implement input validation.

features.name: Template Injection
features.description: Tests for server-side template injection vulnerabilities in templating engines.
verification_steps: 1. Identify template engines used (Twig, Smarty, Blade)
2. Test template parameters with injection payloads
4. Check for remote code execution via templates
7. Check for information disclosure via templates
features.remediation: Sanitize template inputs. Use sandboxed template environments. Keep template engines updated. Implement proper template context separation.

features.name: Email Header Injection
features.description: Tests for email header injection vulnerabilities in contact forms and email functionality.
verification_steps: 1. Test contact form fields with newlines and headers
3. Test subject and body fields for injection
features.remediation: Validate email inputs. Remove newlines from headers. Use secure email libraries. Implement rate limiting on email functions. Regular email security testing.

features.name: Password Reset Vulnerability
features.description: Tests for password reset functionality vulnerabilities including token leakage, weak tokens, and lack of rate limiting.
verification_steps: 4. Verify rate limiting on reset requests
5. Test for token leakage in logs or referrers
6. Check if old tokens are invalidated
7. Test account enumeration via reset function
features.remediation: Use cryptographically secure tokens. Implement short expiration times. Add rate limiting. Invalidate old tokens. Secure token transmission.

features.name: Account Takeover via Email Change
features.description: Tests for insecure email change functionality that could lead to account takeover.
verification_steps: 2. Check for weak email change tokens
3. Test if password required for email change
5. Test for race conditions in email change
6. Check if old email can still be used
7. Test account recovery after email change
features.remediation: Require password for email changes. Send confirmation to old email. Use secure tokens. Implement delay before email change takes effect.

features.name: Session Fixation
features.description: Tests for session fixation vulnerabilities where attackers can force users to use a known session ID.
verification_steps: 1. Test if session ID changes after login
2. Check if session ID can be set via URL parameter
3. Test cookie injection for session fixation
4. Verify session regeneration on privilege change
5. Test with different session initialization methods
7. Test session binding to IP/user agent
features.remediation: Regenerate session ID on login. Don't accept session IDs from URLs. Bind sessions to IP/user agent. Implement proper session management.

features.name: Session Timeout
features.description: Tests for proper session timeout implementation to prevent session hijacking.
verification_steps: 3. Check if sessions expire on browser close
6. Test session persistence after password change
7. Check for session invalidation on logout
features.remediation: Implement both idle and absolute timeouts. Destroy sessions properly on logout. Invalidate sessions after password change. Secure remember me functionality.

features.name: Concurrent Session Control
features.description: Tests for controls preventing multiple concurrent sessions for the same user.
verification_steps: 1. Test logging in from multiple devices/browsers
2. Check if old sessions are invalidated
features.remediation: Implement concurrent session limits. Invalidate old sessions on new login. Track active sessions. Provide admin controls for session management.

features.name: Cookie Security Attributes
features.description: Tests for secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite attributes on authentication cookies.
verification_steps: 1. Check cookie flags: Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite
2. Verify authentication cookies have proper attributes
7. Use browser developer tools for cookie analysis
features.remediation: Set Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict cookies. Use proper cookie scope. Consider __Host- prefix for extra security. Regular cookie security review.

features.name: Sensitive Data in URL
features.description: Tests for sensitive data exposure through URL parameters (session tokens, passwords, etc.).
verification_steps: 1. Check for session IDs in URLs
2. Look for password reset tokens in URLs
3. Test for API keys in query parameters
4. Check referrer headers for sensitive data
6. Verify server logs don't contain sensitive URLs
7. Use automated scanners for URL parameter analysis
features.remediation: Use POST instead of GET for sensitive data. Avoid tokens in URLs. Implement proper session management. Clean URLs of sensitive data. Use HTTP referrer policy.

features.name: Sensitive Data in Logs
features.description: Tests for sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers being logged in cleartext.
verification_steps: 1. Check application logs for sensitive data
5. Test with credit card numbers, passwords in forms
features.remediation: Implement data masking in logs. Don't log sensitive data. Secure log file storage and permissions. Regular log auditing. Use log encryption.

features.name: Data Encryption at Rest
features.description: Tests for encryption of sensitive data stored in databases or file systems.
verification_steps: 1. Check database for encrypted sensitive fields
2. Verify file system encryption for sensitive files
6. Test for plaintext data in backups
7. Check WordPress database for unencrypted sensitive data
features.remediation: Encrypt sensitive database fields. Use file system encryption for sensitive files. Implement proper key management. Use strong encryption algorithms. Regular encryption audits.

features.name: Data Encryption in Transit
features.description: Tests for proper TLS/SSL implementation for data transmission.
verification_steps: 4. Verify TLS version and cipher suites
features.remediation: Enforce HTTPS everywhere. Implement HSTS. Use strong TLS configurations. Regular SSL certificate renewal. Monitor for mixed content.

features.name: Weak Cryptographic Algorithms
features.description: Tests for use of weak or deprecated cryptographic algorithms and protocols.
verification_steps: 1. Check for MD5, SHA1 hash usage
2. Test for weak password hashing algorithms
features.remediation: Use strong hashing algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2). Deprecate weak algorithms. Use modern TLS configurations. Avoid custom crypto implementations.

features.name: Insecure Random Number Generation
features.description: Tests for insecure random number generation for tokens, passwords, or cryptographic operations.
verification_steps: 4. Check for use of `rand()` instead of cryptographically secure functions
features.remediation: Use cryptographically secure random functions (`random_bytes()`, `openssl_random_pseudo_bytes()`). Ensure sufficient entropy. Test randomness of generated values.

features.name: Business Logic Vulnerabilities
features.description: Tests for business logic flaws including price manipulation, quantity manipulation, and workflow bypass.
verification_steps: 1. Test for price manipulation in e-commerce
4. Verify access controls in multi-step processes
5. Test for time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) issues
features.remediation: Implement server-side validation of business rules. Use atomic transactions. Validate all client-submitted data. Implement proper workflow controls. Regular business logic testing.

features.name: Race Conditions
features.description: Tests for race condition vulnerabilities in concurrent operations like balance transfers or inventory management.
verification_steps: 2. Check inventory management under concurrent access
3. Test vote/like systems for race conditions
4. Verify account creation with same details
5. Use automated race condition testing tools
features.remediation: Use database transactions with proper isolation levels. Implement optimistic/pessimistic locking. Use atomic operations. Test under high concurrency.

features.name: API Rate Limiting
features.description: Tests for proper rate limiting on API endpoints to prevent abuse and DoS attacks.
verification_steps: 1. Test API endpoints without rate limits
2. Verify different rate limits for authenticated vs anonymous users
3. Check for burst limits and sustained limits
features.remediation: Implement comprehensive rate limiting. Use different tiers for different user types. Include burst protection. Return proper rate limit headers. Monitor for abuse.

features.name: Denial of Service (DoS) Protection
features.description: Tests for DoS protection mechanisms including request limiting, resource limits, and timeout handling.
verification_steps: 4. Test for memory exhaustion via large inputs
7. Verify DoS protection at network/application level
features.remediation: Implement resource limits. Use timeouts on operations. Configure web server for DoS protection. Use CDN/WAF for DDoS protection. Monitor resource usage.

features.name: Web Application Firewall (WAF) Bypass
features.description: Tests for techniques to bypass WAF protections including encoding, fragmentation, and HTTP parameter pollution.
verification_steps: 1. Test SQLi payloads with various encodings
3. Test with mixed case and encoding
features.remediation: Keep WAF rules updated. Implement multi-layer security. Monitor WAF logs for bypass attempts. Regular WAF testing and tuning. Use behavioral analysis.

features.name: Input Validation Bypass
features.description: Tests for techniques to bypass input validation including null bytes, unicode, and case manipulation.
verification_steps: 5. Test with extra whitespace and newlines
6. Verify validation on client vs server
7. Use fuzzing for input validation testing
features.remediation: Implement consistent server-side validation. Normalize inputs before validation. Use allowlists over blocklists. Test validation with various encodings.

features.name: Authentication Bypass

verification_steps: 1. Test for SQL injection in login forms
6. Test cookie manipulation for auth bypass
features.remediation: Implement secure authentication mechanisms. Remove default accounts/credentials. Regular credential audits. Monitor for authentication anomalies. Code review for backdoors.

features.name: Privilege Escalation

features.name: Mass Assignment

features.remediation: Use allowlists for assignable fields. Implement proper data binding. Validate all input fields. Use framework security features for mass assignment protection.

features.name: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)

features.name: File Path Traversal

features.remediation: Validate and sanitize file paths. Use basename() to extract filenames. Implement allowlists for accessible files. Use chroot or similar restrictions.

features.remediation: Validate file type by content, not extension. Use allowlists for file types. Store files outside web root. Scan uploaded files for malware. Set proper permissions.

features.name: Server-Side Validation Bypass

6. Test with malformed data after validation
